model_narrative: We run a set of canning plants that ship a product to a set of markets. For every plant-to-market pair we decide how many cases to ship. Shipping a case over a route costs a freight rate that scales with the distance of that route, so longer routes are more expensive per case. The objective is to choose the shipment quantities that make the total transportation cost as small as possible.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the canning plants that act as supply sources

sets.name: j
sets.doc: the markets that act as demand destinations

params.name: a
params.doc: the production capacity available at each plant, in cases

params.name: b
params.doc: the demand that must be met at each market, in cases

params.name: d
params.doc: the distance from each plant to each market, in thousands of miles

params.name: f
params.doc: the freight rate, in dollars per case per thousand miles

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the quantity shipped from each plant to each market, in cases

vars.name: z
vars.doc: the total transportation cost over all shipments, in thousands of dollars

constraints.name: z
constraints.intent: The total transportation cost accumulates the cost of every shipment across all plant-to-market routes. For each route, the cost of moving a case is the freight rate applied over that route's distance, and the route's total cost is that per-case cost times the number of cases shipped on it. Set the total cost equal to the sum of these route costs over every plant and market.

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Each plant can only ship as much as it is able to produce. For each plant, the total amount shipped from it to all the markets must not exceed that plant's available capacity.

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Every market's requirement must be covered. For each market, the total amount shipped into it from all the plants must be at least that market's demand.

query: We're looking at a load-spreading guideline so we don't lean too hard on any one lane: no single plant-to-market route should carry more than 250 cases. What does the optimizer return for the cost and the routing under that rule?

query: We're looking at a load-balancing idea for the Seattle cannery: cap how much it pushes toward the two inland markets combined — the Chicago and Topeka destinations together — at 200 cases, so it doesn't over-commit to the interior. What does the optimizer return for the total cost and the routing under that rule?

query: There's a two-part proposal on the table for the next planning cycle: thin out the heavily loaded Seattle-to-Chicago lane so it carries no more than 200 cases, and at the same time pull San Diego back from leaning so hard on Topeka by holding its Topeka shipment to 200 cases or under. Run the pair together and walk me through the resulting cost and shipment mix.

query: Topeka leans entirely on far-southern San Diego while Seattle never touches it. Shouldn't Seattle be carrying at least as much of the Topeka demand as San Diego does, so that corridor isn't single-sourced? What's keeping the model from balancing the two plants on Topeka?

query: San Diego is hauling a big load all the way up to New York while it's also our southern supplier, and Seattle barely shows up in that eastern market. Wouldn't it be more balanced to keep San Diego's New York shipment under two-fifths of all the cases flowing into New York — that is, under 40 percent of the sum of what Seattle and San Diego each send to New York — and let Seattle carry more? What's the model seeing that I'm not?

query: New York's order gets split very lopsidedly between the two plants right now. Shouldn't every plant be on the hook for a real slice of that demand — say each cannery, Seattle and San Diego alike, ships at least 120 cases into New York — so we're not over-relying on one source? Walk me through what's pulling it the other way.

query: Several plant-to-market lanes sit completely empty in the current plan, which leaves us exposed if any single route gets disrupted. Couldn't we just require every lane to stay warm — each plant putting at least 50 cases on every market it could serve? What's the tradeoff I'm missing by dual-sourcing every market like that?
